findings.raw_finding: Core OS commands may be too hard to discover because Search, Share, and Settings live in hidden Charms rather than visible controls.
findings.evidence: The surface specifies hidden Charms for generic commands such as Search, Share, and Settings.
findings.recommendation: Expose persistent or contextually visible entry points for Search, Settings, and app commands, especially during first use and in desktop workflows.
findings.nearest_confuser: navigation-discoverability, because the risk is also about familiar destinations moving; hidden-feature-discoverability is closer because the commands are explicitly hidden off-screen.
findings.validation_path: Run first-use task tests asking users to find settings, search within an app, and share content without coaching; measure time, failed attempts, and help-seeking.
findings.stop_or_repair_rule: Drop if telemetry and usability tests show most novice and experienced users discover Charms quickly without instruction; revise if only one command, not the whole Charms pattern, causes failure.

findings.raw_finding: The dual Start screen and desktop model may create a split mental model where users cannot predict which environment they are in or where a task should be completed.
findings.evidence: The surface combines a tile-based Start screen optimized for touch with a traditional desktop environment for legacy PC work.
findings.recommendation: Clarify mode boundaries with visible orientation cues, consistent navigation back to Start and desktop, and explicit transitions when launching desktop versus Store apps.
findings.nearest_confuser: register-fighting, because two interaction languages compete visually; information-architecture-overload is stronger because the core risk is maintaining a coherent OS mental model.
findings.validation_path: Observe users switching between launching apps, finding files, changing settings, and returning to prior work; track wrong-environment errors and repeated backtracking.
findings.stop_or_repair_rule: Downgrade if users reliably describe the two environments and choose the right one after a short first-use period.

findings.raw_finding: The launch surface likely violates experienced Windows users' expectations by reducing visible multi-window affordances and shifting common work into full-screen or touch-first patterns.
findings.evidence: The surface notes reduced visible multi-window affordances compared with prior desktop Windows and personas include experienced PC users with existing Windows habits.
findings.recommendation: Preserve visible windowing, task switching, and side-by-side affordances for desktop users; provide migration cues where prior Windows workflows changed.
findings.nearest_confuser: operator-density-undershoot, because reduced multi-window support also hurts expert throughput; interaction-cost-regression is closer because the risk is regression from established Windows habits.
findings.validation_path: Benchmark common legacy workflows against prior Windows versions: open multiple documents, compare windows, switch apps, and recover minimized or obscured work.
findings.stop_or_repair_rule: Drop if objective task times and error rates do not regress for experienced users on common multi-window tasks.

findings.raw_finding: Full-screen, low-density Windows Store apps may underserve users doing frequent PC work who need scanability, comparison, and parallel context.
findings.evidence: The surface describes Windows Store apps that emphasized full-screen, low-density layouts and reduced visible multi-window affordances.
findings.recommendation: Offer denser layouts, split views, keyboard-first paths, and clear support for side-by-side work in apps used for productivity or repeated tasks.
findings.nearest_confuser: interaction-cost-regression, because prior desktop workflows may become slower; operator-density-undershoot is closer for the low-density full-screen app pattern itself.
findings.validation_path: Test productivity tasks requiring comparison, copying between apps, and repeated navigation; compare completion time and context loss against desktop equivalents.
findings.stop_or_repair_rule: Downgrade for consumption-only apps where full-screen low-density layouts improve focus without blocking common tasks.

findings.raw_finding: Hidden generic commands and reduced visible affordances weaken visibility of system status and available actions, making users rely on memory instead of recognition.
findings.evidence: The surface includes hidden Charms and reduced visible multi-window affordances compared with prior desktop Windows.
findings.recommendation: Add visible affordances for currently available commands, app state, open windows, and navigation exits; use progressive hints only as a supplement.
findings.nearest_confuser: hidden-feature-discoverability, because Charms are hidden; navigation-discoverability better covers the broader loss of visible wayfinding and status cues.
findings.validation_path: Ask users what actions are available on each surface and where they are in the system; compare perceived affordances with actual available commands.
findings.stop_or_repair_rule: Revise if failures cluster only around Charms rather than broader navigation and status recognition.

findings.raw_finding: User control and recovery may be weakened when users enter full-screen Store apps or the Start screen without obvious exits, window controls, or escape paths.
findings.evidence: The surface describes full-screen Windows Store apps and reduced visible multi-window affordances compared with prior desktop Windows.
findings.recommendation: Provide consistently visible or easily discoverable exit, switch, close, and return controls across Start, Store apps, and desktop contexts.
findings.nearest_confuser: navigation-discoverability, because exit paths may be hard to find; agency-removed is suggested because the user may feel trapped or unable to undo navigation.
findings.validation_path: Run recovery tasks: accidentally open a Store app, return to prior desktop work, close the app, and find the previous window without instruction.
findings.stop_or_repair_rule: Drop if users can consistently recover from accidental launches and mode switches using visible controls or familiar keyboard paths.

findings.raw_finding: The touch-optimized Start screen may mismatch expectations for mouse-and-keyboard PC users, especially experienced users carrying prior Windows habits into launch.
findings.evidence: The surface says the Start screen is tile-based and optimized for touch, while personas include experienced PC users with existing Windows habits.
findings.recommendation: Adapt affordances by input context: visible mouse targets, keyboard navigation, desktop-oriented defaults, and first-run guidance for changed Start behavior.
findings.nearest_confuser: interaction-cost-regression, because existing workflows may take more effort; navigation-discoverability is closer because the expectation mismatch centers on finding and using navigation.
findings.validation_path: Segment tests by touch users and mouse-keyboard users; compare app launch, search, settings, and task-switching success rates.
findings.stop_or_repair_rule: Downgrade if mouse-keyboard users perform as well as touch users after minimal exposure.

findings.raw_finding: Accessibility risk is likely around hidden commands and mode changes: keyboard and assistive-technology users may not receive enough visible or structural cues to discover commands or recover context.
findings.evidence: The supplied surface includes hidden Charms, full-screen Store apps, and reduced visible multi-window affordances, but does not specify keyboard focus behavior or assistive technology semantics.
findings.recommendation: Audit keyboard traversal, focus order, screen-reader announcements, and command discoverability across Start, Charms, Store apps, and desktop transitions.
findings.nearest_confuser: hidden-feature-discoverability, because the strongest evidence is hidden commands; accessibility-focus-management is a tentative issue-class suggestion pending implementation evidence.
findings.validation_path: Test with keyboard-only and screen-reader users completing search, settings, app switching, closing, and returning-to-desktop tasks.
findings.stop_or_repair_rule: Drop or reclassify if implementation provides reliable keyboard shortcuts, focus restoration, and screen-reader announcements despite the hidden visual model.
